metadata.programme: Om Saravana Bhava Global Research Programme

confidence_legend.MEDIUM: Some primary sources or strong scholarly consensus
confidence_legend.LOW: Limited sources, traditional accounts, or disputed

evidence_legend.Historically documented: Attested in primary epigraphic, archaeological, or literary sources
evidence_legend.Traditional belief: Attested in devotional literature, hagiography, or oral tradition
evidence_legend.Scholarly interpretation: Reconstruction based on analysis by peer-reviewed scholars
evidence_legend.Source required: Claim made in secondary literature without primary source verification

primary_deity.name: Dhandayuthapani
primary_deity.form: Murugan as youthful ascetic (Bala Dhandayuthapani) holding a staff (dhandam)

temple_history.claim: Mentioned as Thiruaavinankudi in Sangam literature Tirumurukāṟṟuppaṭai
Region known as Vaikavurnadu / Vaiyapuri Nadu in Purananuru; ruled by Velir chieftains
Place called Pothini (Pon-udai-nedu-nagar) in Akananuru, ruled by Velir chieftain Neduvel Avi
Patiṟṟuppattu mentions Velir chieftain Velavikkopaduman with matrimonial relations to Cheras
Main idol created by Siddhar Bogar from navapashanam (nine herbal amalgam)
temple_history.source: Temple tradition; Bogar 7000 (devotional text, not independently datable)

temple_history.claim: Original temple structure built by Chera dynasty between 2nd and 5th centuries CE
temple_history.source: Secondary sources cite tradition; no specific ASI excavation report verifying this dating for the structural temple was located

temple_history.claim: Chola dynasty expanded temple and endowed land grants in 10th century CE; Tamil inscriptions on temple walls record grants and tax exemptions
temple_history.source: Multiple secondary sources reference Chola inscriptions at Palani; ASI epigraphic surveys confirm presence of Chola-era Tamil inscriptions

temple_history.claim: Pandya kings, including Jatavarman Sundara Pandyan, enhanced the shrine in 13th-14th centuries; epigraphs detail endowments
temple_history.source: Pandya inscriptions referenced in temple history literature

temple_history.claim: Vijayanagara Empire provided patronage from 14th to 16th centuries
temple_history.source: General historical consensus on Vijayanagara temple patronage in Tamil Nadu

temple_history.claim: Madurai Nayaks administered temple through palayakkarar system from 16th to 18th centuries
During Tirumalai Nayak period, puja rights transferred from Pulippani Siddhar descendants to Brahmin priests
temple_history.source: Temple tradition; no independent epigraphic verification located

temple_history.claim: 1832 copper-plate inscription documents Paraiyar community endowment for daily abhishekam and garlands to Dhandayuthapani
temple_history.source: Copper plate in Metropolitan Museum of Art, New York; deciphered by Thiru Narayanamurthy (2024); reported in The Hindu and Swarajya Magazine

architecture.vimanam: Gold-plated tower above sanctum with sculpted deities

inscriptions.description: 1832 CE copper-plate endowment by Paraiyar community
Chola-period Tamil inscriptions on temple walls recording land grants
inscriptions.note: Specific ASI inscription numbers not located in this search

inscriptions.description: Pandya-period inscriptions recording endowments for festivals and maintenance
inscriptions.note: Specific ASI inscription numbers not located in this search

official_publications.title: HR&CE Tamil Nadu Temple Administration Records
official_publications.publisher: Hindu Religious and Charitable Endowments Department, Government of Tamil Nadu

bibliography.citation: Thiru Narayanamurthy, decipherment of 1832 Palani copper-plate inscription, reported in The Hindu (2024) and Swarajya Magazine (2024)
ASI Epigraphic surveys of Tamil Nadu temple inscriptions
bibliography.note: Specific Palani inscription numbers require further ASI archive consultation

rights.value: Managed by HR&CE Department, Government of Tamil Nadu

entities.overall_confidence: HIGH for location and documented inscriptions; MEDIUM for dynastic chronology; LOW for origins and traditional narratives

entities.canonical_tamil: அகல்வினை யுட்சார் சட்சம யிகளொடு வெட்கா தட்கிடு ஓம் ஐங்கரன் தம்பியே போற்றி

variants.variant: agalvinai yutsAr satchama yikaLodu vetkA thatkidu

prosody.note: Requires consultation of Thiruppugazh prosody commentaries

historical_evidence.source: Accepted scholarly attribution; temple tradition corroborates

rights.value: Public domain (author deceased >500 years); digital rights vary by publisher

entities.king: NOT_VERIFIED (endowment by Paraiyar community elders, not royal)

date.era: Salivahana era year mentioned (disputed by decipherer)

publication.primary: Metropolitan Museum of Art, New York (custodian)

translation.summary: Endowment deed by Paraiyar community (Thiruvalluvar gotra, four clans: Veda-muhan, Jala-muhan, Puli-muhan, Karadi-muhan) for daily thirumanjanam (abhishekam) and thirumalaichathu (flower garland) to Dhandayuthapani Swami. Community leaders include Palaya Swamiyar, Pachha Kandaiyer, Chinnappa Nayackar. Kulaguru: Gnana Sambandamurthy. Invocation from Periya Puranam.

entities.summary: Early 19th-century copper-plate inscription documenting Scheduled Community (Paraiyar) patronage of Palani temple, challenging stereotypes about temple access. Records systematic community fundraising (including marriage contributions) for perpetual worship services.

institution.current_custodian: Metropolitan Museum of Art, New York

entities.notes: Full academic epigraphic edition pending. Decipherment by citizen archaeologist; requires peer-reviewed epigraphic verification.

period.source: Multiple scholarly estimates; no definitive epigraphic date of birth located

historical_evidence.claim: Author of Thiruppugazh, a corpus of approximately 1300 songs on Lord Murugan
Composed songs on all Six Abodes (Arupadai Veedu) of Murugan including Palani (songs 104-200)

traditional_biography.claim: Born in Tiruvannamalai; lived a dissolute youth; attempted suicide at Arunachaleswarar temple; saved by Murugan; thereafter composed Thiruppugazh
traditional_biography.source: Hagiographic tradition; no contemporary epigraphic or documentary evidence

entities.name: Thai-Poosam
entities.significance: Most important festival at Palani; Kavadi attam (carrying ceremonial burdens); Paal Kudam (milk pots); millions of pilgrims
entities.historical_attestation: Temple inscriptions and HR&CE records document festival endowments from medieval period to present

relationships.description: Arunagirinathar composed Thiruppugazh songs 104-200 on Palani Dhandayuthapani
1832 copper-plate inscription documents Paraiyar community endowment for Palani temple worship
Thai-Poosam is the primary festival celebrated at Palani temple
